title: Write about the following topic:
prompt: In the future, nobody will buy printed newspapers or books because they will be able to read everything they want online without paying. To what extent do you agree or disagree with this statement?

paragraphs.text: Write about the following topic:
In the future, nobody will buy printed newspapers or books because they will be able to read everything they want online without paying. To what extent do you agree or disagree with this statement?
paragraphs.italic_text: In the future, nobody will buy printed newspapers or books because they will be able to read everything they want online without paying. To what extent do you agree or disagree with this statement?

paragraphs.text: Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.
Write at least 250 words.
Topic: printed book / electronic book
Task: read everything online + nobody will buy printed book
1: can find sth but not everything
High-quality content (subscribe)
2: many printed book lover
Professional book
(Writers still publishing printed books)mostly disagree (sales may decrease)
In the future, people will no longer buy printed books or newspapers. Instead, they can read everything online for free.
It is often argued that printed books and newspapers will disappear in the future. Instead, people will be able to access most reading materials online without cost.
paragraphs.italic_text: It is often argued that printed books and newspapers will disappear in the future. Instead, people will be able to access most reading materials online without cost.

paragraphs.text: This essay will discuss this opinion and argue that it is largely inaccurate.
This essay argues that this view is largely inaccurate.
paragraphs.italic_text: This essay argues that this view is largely inaccurate.

paragraphs.text: Since we are in the middle of the golden age of the Internet, it is simple to acquire all kinds of information online.
Although the Internet has made information widely accessible, not all content is freely available.
paragraphs.italic_text: Although the Internet has made information widely accessible, not all content is freely available.

paragraphs.text: However, a huge amount of high-quality content on the Internet is not for free.
However, a significant proportion of high-quality online content is behind paywalls.
paragraphs.italic_text: However, a significant proportion of high-quality online content is behind paywalls.

paragraphs.text: Many content creators choose to protect their works and profit from them by charging a fee.
There are also laws which protect the author's rights.
In addition, copyright laws safeguard authors’ intellectual property.
paragraphs.italic_text: In addition, copyright laws safeguard authors’ intellectual property.

paragraphs.text: For instance, many platforms now allow key opinion leaders to create subscribe communities, where they share their distinctive ideas.
For example, many platforms allow creators to establish subscription-based communities where they share exclusive content.
paragraphs.italic_text: For example, many platforms allow creators to establish subscription-based communities where they share exclusive content.

paragraphs.text: Normal users have to pay in order to access this information.
Users must pay to access this material.
paragraphs.italic_text: Users must pay to access this material.

paragraphs.text: As a result, people can indeed find some of what they want on the web, but not everything.
It is true that the emergence of electronic books may decrease the sales of printed newspapers or books, but printed materials are still an essential need.
Many students and professionals in fields such as medicine and law rely on printed books as references for their papers, as many of these materials are not available in digital form.
Students and professionals in fields such as medicine and law often rely on printed texts as references, as some materials are not available digitally.
paragraphs.italic_text: Students and professionals in fields such as medicine and law often rely on printed texts as references, as some materials are not available digitally.

paragraphs.text: Moreover, many book lovers and collectors continue to buy printed books, mostly out of their love for the physical form.

In conclusion, although people have access to almost everything they want and the market of printed materials is shrinking, many high-quality contents and professional books are paid-only and cannot be found online. (252 words)
In conclusion, although access to online information is expanding and the market for printed materials is declining, many high-quality resources remain paid and are not fully available online.
paragraphs.italic_text: In conclusion, although access to online information is expanding and the market for printed materials is declining, many high-quality resources remain paid and are not fully available online.

paragraphs.text: What you ALREADY have
Clear position (you disagree)
Logical paragraphing
Relevant examples
Good range of ideas
What you need
1. Grammar precision (important!!!)
Small errors lower your score
2. Academic tone
Use:
a significant proportion
remains limited
subscription-based
3. Lexical upgrade
Replace basic phrases with:
behind paywalls
intellectual property
exclusive content
4. More precise claims
Use:
in many contexts
to a large extent

It is often claimed that printed books and newspapers will eventually become obsolete, as people will be able to access all reading materials online free of charge. However, I strongly disagree with this view, as it overlooks both the economic realities of content creation and the enduring value of printed media.

Admittedly, the Internet has revolutionised access to information, making a vast amount of content readily available. Nevertheless, it is unrealistic to assume that everything can be accessed without cost. A considerable proportion of high-quality material is restricted by paywalls, as creators and publishers seek to monetise their work. Moreover, copyright regulations play a crucial role in protecting intellectual property, thereby limiting the free distribution of content. For instance, many digital platforms now operate on subscription-based models, offering exclusive articles, research, or insights that are only accessible to paying users. This demonstrates that, despite the abundance of free information, a large share of valuable content remains commercialised.

In addition, it is unlikely that printed materials will disappear entirely, even in an increasingly digital world. In academic and professional contexts, such as medicine and law, printed texts are often regarded as more reliable and authoritative sources. Furthermore, physical books offer a tangible reading experience that many individuals continue to value. Book collectors and enthusiasts, in particular, appreciate the aesthetic and sentimental qualities of printed editions, which digital formats cannot replicate. As a result, demand for printed materials, although reduced, is likely to persist.

In conclusion, while digital technology has significantly expanded access to information, it is improbable that all content will become freely available or that printed books and newspapers will vanish altogether. Both economic factors and reader preferences suggest that traditional and digital formats will continue to coexist. (288)

model_essay_paragraphs: It is true that the emergence of electronic books may decrease the sales of printed newspapers or books, but printed materials are still an essential need.
Many students and professionals in fields such as medicine and law rely on printed books as references for their papers, as many of these materials are not available in digital form.
Students and professionals in fields such as medicine and law often rely on printed texts as references, as some materials are not available digitally.
Moreover, many book lovers and collectors continue to buy printed books, mostly out of their love for the physical form.

In conclusion, although people have access to almost everything they want and the market of printed materials is shrinking, many high-quality contents and professional books are paid-only and cannot be found online. (252 words)
In conclusion, although access to online information is expanding and the market for printed materials is declining, many high-quality resources remain paid and are not fully available online.

It is often claimed that printed books and newspapers will eventually become obsolete, as people will be able to access all reading materials online free of charge. However, I strongly disagree with this view, as it overlooks both the economic realities of content creation and the enduring value of printed media.

Admittedly, the Internet has revolutionised access to information, making a vast amount of content readily available. Nevertheless, it is unrealistic to assume that everything can be accessed without cost. A considerable proportion of high-quality material is restricted by paywalls, as creators and publishers seek to monetise their work. Moreover, copyright regulations play a crucial role in protecting intellectual property, thereby limiting the free distribution of content. For instance, many digital platforms now operate on subscription-based models, offering exclusive articles, research, or insights that are only accessible to paying users. This demonstrates that, despite the abundance of free information, a large share of valuable content remains commercialised.

In addition, it is unlikely that printed materials will disappear entirely, even in an increasingly digital world. In academic and professional contexts, such as medicine and law, printed texts are often regarded as more reliable and authoritative sources. Furthermore, physical books offer a tangible reading experience that many individuals continue to value. Book collectors and enthusiasts, in particular, appreciate the aesthetic and sentimental qualities of printed editions, which digital formats cannot replicate. As a result, demand for printed materials, although reduced, is likely to persist.

In conclusion, while digital technology has significantly expanded access to information, it is improbable that all content will become freely available or that printed books and newspapers will vanish altogether. Both economic factors and reader preferences suggest that traditional and digital formats will continue to coexist. (288)
